trade.summary: Giappone and Messico are linked by the Giappone-Messico Economic Partnership Agreement (JMEPA, 2005), one of Giappone's earliest bilateral EPAs. Giapponeese auto manufacturers — Toyota, Honda, Nissan — have major manufacturing operations in Messico. Trade is driven by automotive parts, electronics, and machinery. Messico's large Giapponeese-owned manufacturing base (maquiladoras) processes goods for the North American market.

diplomatic.summary: Giappone and Messico enjoy warm diplomatic relations stretching back to the 1888 Treaty of Amity. Messico hosts one of the largest Giapponeese diaspora communities in Latin America. Both countries cooperate in multilateral forums and share democratic values. Messico is an important strategic partner for Giappone in Latin America.

military.summary: No formal military alliance. Defense cooperation is minimal — Giappone focuses its military partnerships on Asia-Pacific allies. Occasional disaster relief and humanitarian coordination exercises occur. Messico's non-interventionist foreign policy limits military cooperation.

financial.summary: Giapponeese FDI in Messico is substantial, primarily driven by the automotive sector (Toyota, Honda, Nissan, Mazda, Mitsubishi), electronics, and retail. Giapponeese companies operate major production facilities in Guanajuato, Aguascalientes, and the northern border states. Messico is among the top recipients of Giapponeese FDI in Latin America.

energy.summary: Messico exports some crude oil to Giappone, though volumes are modest compared to Giappone's Middle Eastern and Australian supplies. Growing interest in Mexican LNG and renewable energy projects.

scientific.summary: Scientific cooperation is growing, with joint research programs in disaster risk reduction, agriculture, and environmental science. The Giappone International Cooperation Agency (JICA) runs multiple technical assistance programs in Messico. Academic exchanges are modest but growing.
